body_txt: A story element based on a Real Life object or event (past or present) which is mistaken for fiction by the audience, usually because it seems too unlikely, bizarre, or kitschy to be real . The Trope Namer comes from the 1965 Peanuts TV special A Charlie Brown Christmas , in which Lucy instructs Charlie Brown to "get the biggest aluminum tree you can find [...] Maybe painted pink!" Aluminum Christmas trees? In pink ?! Obvious hyperbole about the artificiality of Christmas, right? Nope: The '50s and The '60s had their share of oddball kitsch, and the aluminum Christmas tree was real , invented in 1955 and first widely distributed in 1959. They were artificial trees with foil needles, not hollow metal cones as seen in the special, but they definitely existed, and you could get them in pink. They were usually called "tinsel trees", which is the name under which they are marketed today. There are many videos on YouTube showing how to set up, restore and repair them. So why do viewers assume these shiny spruces must be fictional? It turns out their unflattering depiction in A Charlie Brown Christmas contributed to their demise. They'd already peaked in popularity around 1963, but the special hurt their reputation even more. Sales plummeted , and tinsel trees largely disappeared by 1971, as plastic trees overtook the artificial tree market by appealing to consumers with a look that was a little more realistic and not overtly synthetic. In short, when a quaint element of Real Life appears in a work of fiction, the audience might mistake it for part of the fiction. The usual reason is lack of familiarity with the object, so it is more likely to trip up those viewing the work from a different perspective (e.g. years after the work was created, or from another culture). And most "aluminum Christmas trees" are, like the original, a bit out-of-the-ordinary and kitschy. Even so, it's immensely funny (to those in the know) when someone dismisses a Real Life element as patently absurd and " unrealistic ". This can also occur in works where the writers did do the research, but the truth they uncovered is so bizarre or surprising that audiences think they must have just made it up. In this case, writers may add a "Not Making This Up" Disclaimer . And of course, just because something once existed doesn't mean that it was commonplace . This is why period pieces which did their homework (all the costumes are reconstructions or composites of documented outfits, for example) can still reveal the cultural assumptions of the time in which they were created. Compare Technology Marches On , "Seinfeld" Is Unfunny , Defictionalization , and Poe's Law . A subtrope of Reality Is Unrealistic , Values Dissonance , and Truth in Television . Can also be used as a means of Shown Their Work . Frequently found in Unintentional Period Pieces . If even the creators were unaware that the "tree" was real, it's Accidentally Correct Writing . Contrast with The Coconut Effect . If the tree didn't exist at the time the story was written but does now , it's Life Imitates Art . If the tree exists now and the audience doesn't realize it also existed then , it's likely a case of Older Than They Think . When this occurs in-universe, it's either Eskimos Aren't Real , or the work will give a "Not Making This Up" Disclaimer . It could simply be a case of Small Reference Pools . noreallife         This led to a bizarre legal loophole for decades: a fully automatic 40mm Bofors autocannon was strictly regulated as a "machine gun" under the 1934 Act, but a bolt-action Boys Anti-Tank Rifle or the semi-automatic Finnish Lahti L-39 (firing massive 20mm rounds) were effectively unregulated as "rifles" because they weren't fully automatic. In fact, before 1968, you could even buy dynamite at local hardware stores or mail-order catalogs like Sears & Roebuck just to clear tree stumps from your yard. There was even an assassination attempt on Ernesto "Che" Guevara at the United Nations with a bazooka! In December 1964, anti-Castro Cuban exiles fired a 3.5-inch bazooka at the United Nations headquarters in New York City while Che Guevara, representing Cuba, was addressing the General Assembly. The rocket was fired from the East River waterfront but missed the UN building, landing in the water  In 1961, the United States Army, despite being legally barred from flying combat jets, secretly spent months testing supersonic fighter jets to see which one was best at blowing things up. The Key West Agreement of 1948 was supposed to be the "Great Divorce" of the military: the Air Force, which officially became its own service following separation from the Army on September 18th, 1947, got the fast, fixed-wing toys as well as most transport aircraft, and the Army was stuck with helicopters and a restricted few transport planes. However, by 1961, the Army was frustrated that Air Force jets were "too fast" to see ground targets, flew “too high” for close air support, and required massive, vulnerable runways. In a move that sounds like a technothriller plot, the Army conducted the Army Fixed-Wing Tactical Transport/Low-Level Mission Evaluation and evaluated the Douglas A-4 Skyhawk, the Northrop N-156 (the prototype for the F-5 Tiger aka Freedom Fighter), and the Italian Fiat G.91 to prove they could operate off-road and provide better support than the Air Force. They even painted "U.S. ARMY" on the side of these jets. The Air Force was furious, viewing this as a blatant attempt by the Army to build its own "Tactical Air Command” and the rivalry extended to transport as well, with the Army successfully fielding the de Havilland Canada CV-2 Caribou, a rugged STOL (Short Take-Off and Landing) transport that the Air Force viewed as an additional blatant mission violation. While the tests proved the Army could technically operate a fleet of fighter jets from grass strips, the Air Force caught wind of the "Shadow Air Force" and threw a bureaucratic fit. The Department of Defense eventually shut the program down, reinforcing the ban. This tension ultimately birthed the Johnson- Mc Connell Agreement, where the Army officially surrendered its dreams of fixed-wing fighters in exchange for the Air Force staying away from armed helicopters and handing over its Caribou fleet to the Air Force who promised to still use the CV-2 Caribou for the Vietnam War only to renege on it and retired the entire fleet as soon as possible. This rivalry also resulted in the rise of the A-10 Thunderbolt II (better known as the Warthog) in response to the Army’s trialing of the Lockheed AH-56 Cheyenne and the debate on close air support still continues to this day.
